title: Chicken tikka masala
subtitle: Charred chicken tikka in a buttery tomato and cream sauce
excerpt: Chicken marinated in yoghurt and spices, grilled or griddled until charred at the edges, then finished in a rich tomato and cream sauce fragrant with garam masala and fenugreek. Britain's most popular curry, descended from the Bangladeshi-British curry-house tradition and now as British as fish and chips.
sourceNotes: Chicken tikka masala is a British-Indian invention of the curry-house era, widely attributed to Bangladeshi-British restaurateurs in the 1970s. It is recorded in numerous British-Indian cookbooks including Madhur Jaffrey's 'Indian Cookery' (1982) and the Cobra Good Curry Guide. The dish was formally recognised as a British national dish in 2001 by the then Foreign Secretary Robin Cook.

content.text: Two things make the difference between a flat curry-house tikka masala and a good one. First, the char on the chicken: it must be cooked on a very hot griddle or under a hot grill until the edges blacken and the marinade caramelises. The smoke and char are not optional. They are the flavour that makes tikka masala taste like it came from a restaurant rather than a jar. Second, the fenugreek leaves: crumbled in at the very end, they give the sauce the specific aromatic note that defines the dish.

The sauce is a simplified makhani (butter sauce) base, onion, ginger, garlic, tomatoes, cream, and garam masala. It should be smooth, deep orange, and rich but not heavy. Blending the sauce completely before adding the chicken is important: the texture should coat the tikka pieces rather than sit around them.

Mix the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{garam-masala}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{chilli-powder}}, {{turmeric}}, and salt together. Toss the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} pieces through the marinade, cover, and refrigerate for at least 4 hours. Heat a griddle pan to maximum heat. Griddle the chicken pieces in batches for 4 to 5 minutes per side until charred at the edges and cooked through. Set aside.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}} for 15 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 10 minutes until reduced. Blend the sauce until completely smooth. Return to the pan, add the {{double-cream}}, {{garam-masala}}, and {{fenugreek-leaves}}, and season with salt. Add the charred chicken pieces and simmer for 5 minutes. Serve with basmati rice or naan.

items.fix: Cook the tomatoes until the sauce reduces to a thick paste before blending. After blending, return to the heat and simmer until the sauce coats the back of a spoon before adding the cream
items.cause: Tomatoes not cooked down enough before blending

items.fix: Use boneless thighs rather than breast. They stay moist with the high heat. Remove from the griddle as soon as cooked through; they will finish cooking in the sauce
items.cause: Overcooked on the griddle or thighs not used

items.fix: Kasoori methi (dried fenugreek leaves) added at the end is what gives curry-house tikka masala its distinctive aroma. Add just before serving and crumble them in your palm to release the fragrance
items.cause: Fenugreek leaves omitted or added too early

items.fix: Cook the tinned tomatoes properly until they darken. Add an extra half teaspoon of chilli powder at the tomato stage
items.symptom: Sauce is pale orange rather than deep red

content.text: Chicken tikka masala is the most ordered dish in British restaurants and has been for decades. It came out of the British-Bangladeshi curry-house tradition that developed in the 1960s and 1970s when Bangladeshi immigrants opened restaurants across Britain, adapting Mughal and North Indian cooking to British tastes. The tikka masala emerged from that adaptation: chicken tikka (a real Indian dish) finished in a tomato-cream sauce developed for British customers who wanted something saucier. Foreign Secretary Robin Cook called it a genuine British national dish in 2001, which prompted predictable debate. The dish sits across the border between borrowed and transformed. It is as British as it is Indian, and that is the whole point.
